So far this month, Cuba has suffered 3 nationwide blackouts — the result of years of underinvestment, mismanagement and, more recently, the U.S. blockade on oil and refined products shipments.

The IMF has expanded a bit its view on how the oil market has so far absorbed the Strait of Hormuz crisis, publishing the blog below. I don't agree with all what the Fund says, but nonetheless interesting. 

 https://t.co/o02zfswTat

Excluding any US-Iran talks about Hormuz, China remains the most important factor for oil prices for the next 60 days or so. All eyes on Beijing.

Ex-China Asian oil imports have recovered to normal levels (see 📈⤵️), within the 2023-2025 range. What China does next is crucial. https://t.co/jJTUd5ZCGR

Last week, I wrote this @Opinion column about what China may do next. I see a recovery in oil imports in July and August, but purchases will remain 25% below pre-war levels. Based on my industry soundings, I don't see China stockpiling in the short-term. https://t.co/aspIn1l5eK

@opinion Important to note that the (very preliminary) data for July 1-14 shows no recovery whatsoever in Chinese oil imports, with seaborne offloadings running at ~5.5m b/d (*below* June average), per @Vortexa data. That's extraordinary for a country that pre-war was buying 10-12m b/d.

Iran appears to be trying to shut down a lifeline of the global oil market -- the "shuttle" tankers that are running from Persian Gulf terminals (mostly UAE ports) to the Gulf of Oman where they rendezvous with other tankers in ship-to-ship operations.  

https://t.co/5PzFVOFmt2

Russia is struggling to sell its crude oil, with floating inventories building up again. Its troubles are compounded by the need to sell overseas a growing amount of barrels as Ukrainian attacks have reduced its domestic refining capacity. 

https://t.co/GJgaEpBr5K

Global Data Centres and Electricity Consumption

Data centres for artificial intelligence and other forms of computing account for a small share of worldwide electricity consumption, according to estimates prepared by the International Energy Agency (IEA) and other researchers.

Consumption is growing very rapidly but from a low base and the extra demand will be dwarfed by increases from industrial users, electric vehicles, domestic appliances and airconditioning over the next decade.

From a global perspective, increased electricity demand from data centres is likely to have only a moderate impact on prices, service and reliability for other users.

But data centres are strongly clustered in a small number of countries, and within them a small number of regions, a pattern that is expected to persist.

In these regions, data centres account for a high and growing share of consumption, having a much greater impact on other users.

Data centres and artificial intelligence are expected to pose a relatively modest challenge for the global energy system in the future.

But managing their local impacts on availability, cost and reliability for other users is already proving a significant challenge ...

This is a short extract from my newsletter emailed to clients earlier today. If you would like to read my newsletters in full, three times a week, you can subscribe via my website https://t.co/aW1Xb1tAI9

CHINA’s petroleum refiners cut their crude processing for the third month running in June as imports continued to be disrupted by the war between Iran and the United States. Refiners processed a total of 51 million tonnes down from 62 million in the same month a year ago and the lowest for the time of year for eight years since 2018:

EIA reported -1.7 million bbls for crude, -3 million bbls for SPR. -4.7 million bbls total. 

Total liquids saw a build of 10.3 million bbls as product exports fell by 1.785 million bbls week over week. 

Implied demand for distillate dropped by 25%, which is likely a timing issue. Distillate inventory saw a build of 4.6 million bbls.

EIA has reported draws below our estimate. Partially attributed to higher than expected US crude oil production, but also related to when those barrels return (storage difference). 

The lag can last 2-4 weeks.

3-2-1 crack spread just hit $68/bbl. 

Congrats Bessent and BOJ 👏

You guys really fucked it up. Now if crude rallies, refining margins won’t fall. You hit the wall sooner and faster. https://t.co/FvtcMTjPXR

Strait of Hormuz over the last 48 hours: Back to the start.

As the war rages on, Fujairah as the main tanker shuttle port is all but dead after the IRGC fired missiles at two VLCCs, the first escalation of its type yesterday.

Even with US protection, it was not enough, and this is going to take ~6 million b/d of flow out of the market.

To further add to my point that the escalation is increasing, this segment from the WSJ article is very concerning:

"There was also concern among shippers that the scope of Iran’s attacks was widening away from the immediate vicinity of the strait after a vessel was targeted off Oman early Tuesday, according to its owner, London-based Stolt-Nielsen. It had not crossed the strait at all and was loaded with Omani chemicals headed to Malaysia, according to Kpler."

We are in no-man's land now. Either Iran controls the Strait of Hormuz or it doesn't. It's all-or-nothing.

I have included the video from @MarineTraffic.

Fujairah is done.

"Sinokor and MSC executives are concerned the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps will continue to attack their ships to try to shut down the shuttle market, said people familiar with the matter. At least two captains on their tankers have refused to go through the strait in recent days."

RE: Hormuz offset flows

An error I still see people making months into tracking Hormuz oil flow is thinking you can add the entire ~7.5 MMbpd of combined Saudi Red Sea + UAE Fujairah flow as an offset to get back to that ~20 MMbpd pre-war Hormuz flow level.

But those offset routes didn't start at zero and were shipping ~3.5 MMbpd of oil prewar.

You get to add ~4 MMbpd to Hormuz flow from offsets, not 7+ MMbpd.

10-day average oil flow exiting Hormuz has fallen to around ~7.5 MMbpd as of the latest count via @Kpler.

That's half the late-June high-water mark when exits surged and stranded Gulf oil-on-water was rapidly drawn down. https://t.co/jTjZskOq2D

EIA weekly petroleum status: US crude inventories fell by 1.7 million barrels, while a further 3 million barrels were released from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR), taking the cumulative post-war drawdown to almost 100 million barrels. Gasoline stocks declined by 1.5 million barrels, with Gulf Coast inventories falling to their lowest level since 2017, highlighting continued regional tightness during the peak summer driving season. Distillate inventories, however, rose by 4.6 million barrels, providing some welcome relief to the segment of the refined products market that has experienced the greatest supply stress in recent months. 
Elsewhere, inventories at the Cushing delivery hub climbed back above 20 million barrels. US crude exports increased to 3.7 million bpd but remained below the one-year average of 4.2 million bpd and well below the record 6.4 million bpd reached in May. 
Domestic crude production was unchanged, while four-week average implied demand for both gasoline and distillates eased, broadly in line with normal seasonal trends.
EIA Crude and Fuel Stock Report https://t.co/DdP6Bfnvvk

Risk sentiment is finding support after US producer price inflation unexpectedly slowed in June, helping #gold recover from early losses to trade back in positive territory. Core PPI, excluding food and energy, rose 4.7% year-on-year, below the 5.1% consensus and down from 4.9% in May, while headline PPI slowed to 5.5% from 6.5%, also undershooting the 6.2% forecast. The softer-than-expected data suggests the inflationary impact from the Iran conflict has, so far, remained relatively contained, easing fears of an even more aggressive Federal Reserve response and providing some support for both risk assets and precious metals.

Gold remains trapped in a broad USD 3,950–4,200 range as investors weigh the inflationary impact of higher energy prices against the longer-term risks to economic growth. https://t.co/5P17TUTKpU via @saxobank https://t.co/0Uncxen5Pv

Chicago and especially Paris #wheat futures have surged by more than 10% over the past week, lifting the September Paris milling wheat contract to a 17-month high above EUR 228 per tonne, a 5.5% gain today alone. The rally reflects a combination of acute geopolitical disruption and an increasingly tight global supply outlook.

The immediate catalyst was a sharp escalation in military attacks around the Sea of Azov, with strikes targeting Russian and Ukrainian grain export infrastructure. Temporary disruptions to shipping through the Don-Azov Channel and renewed attacks on Black Sea export ports raised concerns about exports from a region that accounts for a significant share of global wheat trade.

However, the market was already on edge. Severe drought in the US and Canada, deteriorating crop prospects across Europe, and the USDA's latest downward revisions to production and global ending stocks had already tightened the supply outlook. At the same time, poor European corn crops are expected to boost feed wheat demand, further increasing pressure on available supplies.

In short, weather-related production risks created a highly sensitive market, while the renewed disruption to Black Sea grain logistics provided the spark that triggered the latest leg higher in wheat prices.

A reminder that the real stress in energy markets is not in crude oil but in refined products. While Brent has rallied back above USD 80, diesel, gasoil and jet fuel continue to command exceptionally strong premiums as low inventories, refinery disruptions and peak seasonal demand tighten product availability.

As the charts highlight, crack spreads across the US, Europe and Asia have surged to multi-year highs, signalling that refiners - not crude producers - are currently capturing the bulk of the value. Refining margins are now approaching historic levels, creating a major windfall for operators at a time when global middle-distillate inventories remain critically low and supply chains continue to face disruption. Until refined product stocks begin to rebuild or demand eases seasonally, these elevated margins are likely to keep the energy complex well supported, even if crude supply itself remains relatively comfortable.

#Commodities:

#CrudeOil prices trade higher for a third day after Trump threatened further strikes on Iran, hours after the US resumed its blockade on Iran’s shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. Meanwhile, Trump’s U-turn on plans to impose a 20% charge on shipments through the waterway briefly sent prices lower before the resumption of attacks, including Houthi groups firing ballistic missiles and drones on Saudi Arabia added to concerns about supply from the region. Focus on US stockpiles amid the release of weekly data from the EIA.

#Gold rallied to USD 4,100 after softer-than-expected US CPI data raised expectations for a less aggressive Federal Reserve. However, the move quickly reversed, with prices slipping back toward USD 4,030 after Fed Chair Kevin Warsh signalled monetary policy may still need to be tightened. At the same time, renewed gains in oil prices and fresh US strikes against Iran revived inflation concerns, shifting market focus back toward the risk of higher interest rates rather than the longer-term, gold-supportive economic fallout from a sustained energy shock. For now, gold is sending no clear directional signal, with the yellow metal continuing to consolidate following a prolonged and deep correction.
